observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Services Office (GSO) that in the preparation of the RPCPPE, ensure: (a) The adaption of the classification of PPE accounts used in the accounting records; (b) That the condition of each property item during the physical count are indicated in the report to determine its serviceability and if disposal thereof is proper; and, (c) the inclusion of the Other Infrastructure Assets, Military, Police and Security Equipment, Other Machinery and Equipment, and Books.

Further, we recommended to the GSO to use the format prescribed by Government Accounting Manual in the preparation of RCPPE and to ensure that the report is duly-certified by the Inventory Committee and approved by the Head of the Agency or its authorized representative.

Also, we recommended to ensure that an invitation to witness the conduct the physical count is sent to the COA Audit Team.

Finally, we recommended directing the Municipal Accounting Office and the General Services Office to establish an effective or practical system that would ensure the expeditious and regular reconciliation of records of both offices pertaining to the municipality's Property, Plant and Equipment taking into consideration the guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Office (GSO) submitted additional Inventory Reports for the accounts: Land, Land Improvements, Infrastrucuture Assets, Buildings and Other Structures, and Machinery and Equipment, Transportation Vehicles, and Other Property, Plant and Equipment in reply to the observation. Thus, from net variance of  P632,903,478.85 noted in from previous years’ audit, it was reduced to P14,038,445.21.
observations.reason:
The Report syill lack required data/ information.

The report submitted was not signed by the Inventory Committee and prescribed format was not followed.

Invitation was not sent to the audit team.

Policy was not yet established by the management.

observations.recommendation: Finally, we also recommended the Municipal Mayor to file request for authority to write-off the dormant Other Receivables account to COA duly supported by the documents prescribed under Section 8.0 of the same circular.
observations.managementAction: On the process of gathering the required supporting documents
observations.reason: Certain supporting documents are currently not yet available and on the process of acquisition.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review the information presented in the different financial statements and make adjustments, when necessary, to reconcile the amounts and accounts presented in the financial statements, failing which would render their financial statements as misrepresented which is not in conformance with Section 27 of the International Public-Sector Accounting Standards.
We further recommended disclosing the relevant information as stated in letters “a” to “e” above, in the Notes to Financial Statements to help the end-user understand the Municipality’s financial position and performance.

We recommended that the Mayor instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to:
Effect the adjustment of the accounts as per suggested adjusting entry, viz.
Assign accounting personnel who will focus on the monitoring of the Accounts Receivable pertaining to MonCAST and effecting the necessary adjustments as soon as possible or before year-end.
Ensure that proper accounts, as provided under the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs under COA Circular Nos. 2015-009 and 2016-004, are used in recording the transactions of the Municipality to avoid misrepresentation of the ending balances in the Financial Statement at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
Municipal Engineering Office:
to determine the labor cost attributable to the above-cited infrastructure projects
to maintain a record of the number of workers and the number of hours rendered to each project by those employed under Job Order basis and Contract of Service to account for the attributable labor cost to the infrastructure project; and,
Municipal Accounting Office to coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office in determining the labor cost component of the afore-cited projects and records the same as part of the project cost.
Municipal Accounting Office to ensure that all costs directly attributable to the construction of the infrastructure project are properly accounted for in accordance with IPSAS 17.

We recommended that the Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Facilitate the gathering of the necessary documents required in the derecognition of the properties already under the control of the Barangays or other entities and secure an authority from the Sangguniang Bayan by way of a resolution to authorize the Mayor to execute a Deed of Donation (DOD) or a Memorandum of Agreement (MOA);
Draw a Journal Entry Voucher derecognizing the assets from the books of the Municipality and furnish the respective beneficiaries a copy of the JEV and MOA/DOD to facilitate the recording in the latter’s respective books; and
Provide the Office of the Auditor copies of the JEV together with the supporting documents taking up the derecognition of the above-cited PPE.

We recommended that the Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Implement control measures in the issuance of cash tickets as provided in Section 59 (C) of the Local Treasury Operations Manual to preclude loss or misuse of government funds.
Ensure that Reports of Collections and Deposits for cash tickets collections include information as to the breakdown of collections, the date of collections and the number of cash tickets issued per day.
We also recommended that the Management shall adhere to the laws, rules and regulations as these were promulgated for uniform application and to safeguard government resources.

We recommended that the Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Immediately submit the Disbursements Vouchers and its supporting documents for the months of October to December 2021 and Liquidation Reports for the month of December 2021 under the General Fund.
Install measures that will facilitate the timely submission of the financial statements, Disbursement Vouchers, Official Receipts, Liquidation Reports, and other financial reports within the prescribed periods provided in Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 and Section 7.2.1 (a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineering Office to ensure provision of costs for the tarpaulin signboards for infrastructure projects implemented “by administration” and compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that the Management includes in their budget discussion the long unexpended continuing appropriation balances and declare it as savings; and revert the balances to the unappropriated surplus of the 20% Development Fund for it to be reprogrammed to another vital developmental projects/programs/activities of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Management submit a justification for the non-withholding of the 10% Creditable Withholding Tax on the allowances and/or honorarium paid to judges and public attorneys.
We likewise recommended that the Management instruct the Municipal Accounting Office to deduct the applicable withholding tax on allowances, honorarium, and similar payments subject to withholding tax as required under Section 2.57.2 of RR No. 2-98, as amended by RR No. 11-2018 and RR No. 14-2018.

We recommended that the Mayor direct the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) together with the Municipal Engineering Office (MEO) and other concerned offices to address the root cause of the non-implementation of the programmed development projects and come up with a concrete plan of avoiding the same lapses. The Audit Team shall be furnished with a copy of the plan.
We likewise recommended that the Mayor instruct the MEO to fast track the implementation of the development project that are unimplemented as of December 31, 2021 and see to it that the same are completed in CY 2022.
We also recommended that the Mayor instruct the MPDO to monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development projects in accordance with the approved development plans as provided in Section 476 of Republic Act No. 7160 or the Local Government Code of 1991.

To achieve the objectives of RA 10121 and its IRR, we recommended the following courses of action:
Management must endeavor to strengthen the monitoring and evaluation function of the MDRRM Office and other implementing offices, particularly on the assessment and analysis of the progress in the implementation of the MDRRM Plan.
Ensure inclusion of DRRM PPAs charged under continuing appropriation and the prior year’s unexpended balance in the LDRRMFIP which aids in making timely decisions to ensure that progress is maintained according to schedules and targets.
Maximization of available resources on the local DRRMF for disaster mitigation and preparedness to build the resilience of the Municipality of Monkayo against disasters including climate change impacts.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to provide a written justification for the non-incorporation of the COVID-19-related programs/projects/activities (PPAs) sourced from 20% Development Fund in the Annual Investment Program or in any of the Supplemental Annual Investment Program of the Municipality.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that the source of funds for the appropriation of P428,941.71 was taken from the savings from continuing appropriations of 20% Development Fund of various offices. It has also already been previously included in the prior years AIP/SAIP. That is why they proceeded to include it in Appropriation Ordinance No. 04-2020 even it was not incorporated in the CY2020 AIP/SAIP.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin:

The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to provide a written justification for the non-incorporation of the COVID-19-related programs/projects/ activities (PPAs) sourced from LDRRM Fund in the LDRRMFIP and AIP/SAIP of the Municipality;

The Municipal Budget Officer to specify in the SAAOB if the object of expenditure under the LDRRM Operations pertains to COVID-19-related PPAs; and,

The MDRRM Officer to submit copies of the legal basis for realignment and augmentation of all COVID-19-related funds sourced from the Current/Continuing appropriation of the LDRRM Fund and the use of unspent LDDRM Fund in Special Trust Fund for COVID-19-related PPAs and expenses.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that PPAs cited in this audit observations were prioritized and included in the LDRRM Plan. However, non-incorporation of the PPAs in the AIP/SAIP for the year was not the fault of the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Development Council (MDC) because the MDRRM Office’s requests for the approval of the Proposed COVID-19-related PPAs were not submitted on time.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the implementing office in the granting of regular monitoring and analysis of receivable accounts to ensure collection when due and demandable.
Likewise, the Municipal Accounting Office to conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis and validation of the existence of the receivables as prescribed under Section 7.0 of COA Circular No. 2016-005, dated December 19, 2016 and to recognize impairment loss on Other Receivables accounts.
Finally, we also recommended the Municipal Mayor to file request for authority to write-off the dormant Other Receivables account to  duly supported by the documents prescribed under Section 8.0 of the same circular.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accounting Office to use Weighted Average Method in costing the Supplies and Material Inventories and to maintain the required Supplies Ledger Cards for each commodity/stock recorded under the account as provided under COA rules and regulations.

We recommended the Municipal mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accountant to prepare a schedule on the aging of the loan obligations and to present the Loans Payable – Domestic account in the Statement of Financial Position as current and non-current liabilities according to Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accounting Office, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office and other concerned departments, to determine the status of the implementation of the programs and projects pertaining to fund transfers with non-moving balances. For completed programs and projects, the Municipality should return the unutilized balances to the source agency or to the National Treasury in accordance with laws, rules and regulations. For unimplemented programs and projects, the Offices in charge of the implementation should determine the cause of the delay and formulate alternative action plan to fast track the implementation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Accounting Office (MACCO) to transfer immediately the unexpended 30% QRF and 70% MDRRM Fund – MOOE to the Trust Fund books for the year 2019 and do the same at year-end for the years thereafter in compliance with the accounting and reporting guidelines as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-02, dated September 12, 2012 and Section 21 of RA No. 10121.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Local Finance Committee to ensure that sufficient details of the project and cost in the succeeding appropriation ordinances are provided, most especially projects funded under 20% Development Fund.
We further recommended the submission of the specific authorization from the Sanggunian Bayan to the Local Chief Executive to enter into contracts for the implementation of specific projects whose appropriation are stated in generic terms in the approved Annual and Supplemental Budgets; and for continuing appropriations already stated in generic terms, further authorization from the Sanggunian shall be secured before the project implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor directs the MDRRM Officer to evaluate the cause of non-utilization of the allotments for the PPAs under MDRRM Fund and to submit the findings and recommendations to MDDRM Council for proper action.
Further, we recommended the MDDRM Council to monitor the implementation of the PPAs funded under MDRRMF and evaluate whether there is a need to amend/revise/update the MDRRM Plan as per MDRRMO’s recommendation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Budget Office to prioritize the release of full allotment for the appropriated amount for projects/programs/activities LDDRM Fund to ensure the implementation of the DRRM priority projects/programs/activities and to lessen, if not avoid, the risk for misapplication of the fund.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to enjoin the Municipal Engineering Office to ensure implementation of the priority developmental projects funded under 20% Development Fund within the budget year for an efficient and economical attainment of the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the LGU.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to direct the Project-in-Charge to ensure implementation and completion of the infrastructure project within the year of the receipt of funds from the National Government Agencies and within the fiscal year it was budgeted to avoid possible forfeiture of the funding from the National Government upon full implementation of the Annual Cash-Based Budgeting and to ensure the economy and efficiency of the Municipality in the delivery of basic services and facilities to its constituents.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the MonCAST Management, to:
designate a staff or hire the services of an external consultant who shall possess the necessary expertise and technical competence in formulating a detailed 5-Year Business Plan for the LEE, subject to the availability of funds. The designated staff or external consultant shall present the 5-Year Business plan to the LCE, stressing the importance of directing the LEE operations towards financial self-sufficiency;
assign a responsible local staff who shall prepare the Semi-Annual Financial Self-Sufficiency Review (SAFSSR) and SAFSSR Report. The responsible local staff shall submit and present each SAFSSR report to the LCE and local Sanggunian. Copies shall be furnished to the LEE manager who shall use the results of the reports in improving LEE operations; and
assign a designated staff or external consultant who shall be responsible for the preparation of the Financial Self-Sufficient Evaluation (FSSE) Report, together with an analysis on the financial self-sufficiency situation of the LEE as well as recommendation on whether or not the LEE shall continue to operate and undertake other related actions. The FSSE findings shall be submitted and presented to the LCE and local Sanggunian to serve as basis in arriving at a decision on whether to continue LEE operations.

We recommended to the management the following:

a)	Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of the year be accurately recorded based on the certified list of taxpayers’ amount due and collectible to fairly present the said accounts in the financial statements. 

b)	Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to identify the actual uncollected Real Property and Special Education Fund Taxes, excluding the penalties, to establish the balance of RPT/SET receivable as at yearend. Consequently, the Municipal Accountant make necessary adjustments to present fairly in the financial statements the balance of the said receivable accounts. 

c)	Compel the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to reconcile regularly their records so that any discrepancies noted could be immediately adjusted.

d)	Advice the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to consider the acquisition of computer software to automate the treasury processes, enhance operational quality, ensure regulatory compliance, minimize human errors, and realize on-demand reporting.

Recommendations:

      We recommend to the management the following:

   1. To assign additional personnel  to  the  Accounting  Office  who  will
      handle the segregation of the monthly reports in order to  submit  the
      required financial reports on time.


   2. To direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that  submission  of  the
      monthly reports be within the reglementary period as prescribed  under
      COA Circular No. 95-006 dated May 18, 1995, COA  Circular  No.  96-011
      dated October 2, 1996 and Section 122 of P.D. 1445 in order for us  to
      have a timely review of the financial transactions of your LGU.

Management’s Comments:

      During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer and the  Municipal
Accountant have agreed to create a strategy such as fixed cut-off  dates  on
issuance of checks in order to resolve the problem on the  delay  submission
of reports.  They committed to abide with the regulations on the  submission
of monthly financial reports in 2017.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that management will  assign  an
additional personnel to augment the workforce of the Accounting  Section  in
order for them  to  submit  within  the  reglementary  period  the  required
monthly financial reports.

Recommendation:


      We recommend to the management the following:


     1. To require the Municipal Project  Monitoring  Committee  (MPMC)  to
        monitor the project/program implementation of the funds released to
        barangays that resulted to accumulation of huge balance of the  Due
        from LGU account.


     2. To stop granting financial assistance to barangays who  still  have
        un-liquidated balances.

Management’s Comments:

      The management informed the Audit Team that they already required  the
barangays with un-liquidated balances to return the  unspent  cash/financial
assistance and ensured that they will  not  anymore  release  cash/financial
assistance to barangays with unliquidated cash/financial  assistance  during
the incumbency of the newly elected Local Chief Executive.


COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the LGU should monitor  the
funds released to barangays in order to  achieve  the  objective/purpose  in
which the fund was granted.

Recommendations:

      We recommend  to  the  management  to  make  representation  with  the
Maharlika Charity Foundation for the  refund  of  the  unexpended  financial
assistance balance totaling P165,921.98 by requiring them to close the  bank
account of the LGU where all the financial assistance were deposited.
      We also recommend to ensure that the refund be receipted and deposited
immediately in order that the funds refunded would  be  re-appropriated  for
other program that could benefit the general public.

Management’s Comments:

      The Municipal Mayor had  informed  the  Audit  Team  that  he  already
instructed the Municipal Accountant to make a letter  to  Maharlika  Charity
Foundation for the closing of their accounts  and  for  the  refund  of  the
remaining balance.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the management should  make
representation for the speedy closure of their  bank  account  so  that  the
funds could be used to for other program.

Management’s Comments:

      During the exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer had justified that
 he is certain that the Provincial Government had issued  Official  Receipts
 (ORs) in all their remittances  and  that  the  said  documents  were  just
 misplaced during the segregation of attachments to the DVs. None the  less,
 management committed to exert effort to locate  the  ORs  and  submit  them
 immediately to the Audit Team.  Likewise,  they  committed  to  follow  the
 regulation on the reglementary period of remittance of the 50% share to the
 provincial government.

 Recommendation:

      We recommend to the management to make sure to remit to the provincial
government their 50% share of the Special Education Tax within fifteen  (15)
days of every succeeding month as mandated under Section 9 of  Republic  Act
No. 5447, in order to aid the latter in the funding  and  implementation  of
priority programs and projects under the Special Education Fund and also  to
submit the lacking Official Receipts.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the management should remit
on time the share of the Provincial government share to aid them in  funding
the priority programs of the LSB.

Recommendation:

      We recommend to the management to direct the Local School Board  (LSB)
to strictly adhere to the  limitations  set  forth  on  the  utilization  of
Special Education Fund (SEF) and to refrain from  charging  expenditures  to
SEF for expenses that are not related to the operation  and  maintenance  of
elementary and secondary public schools, in order to avoid  disallowance  in
audit.

Management’s Comments:

      The management in their letter reply to the AOM explained  that  their
interpretation of items  a  to  f  above  were  among  the  extra-curricular
activities referred in  the  premise  of  “co-curricular  activities”  since
those activities were all covered with Memorandum from the officials of  the
DepEd. With  that  incorrect  interpretation,  they  committed  to  strictly
adhere to the recommendations and will advise the Local School Board on  the
matter so that the remaining appropriations allocated for  those  activities
will be realigned to expenses allowed to be charged in SEF.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the LSB should refrain from
utilizing the  SEF  so  as  to  avoid  the  issuance  of  suspension  and/or
disallowance in audit.

Recommendations:


      We recommend to the management to make sure that all requests for  the
procurement of medicines by the Municipality  should  be  supported  with  a
certification signed by the requisitioning officer that  the  drug  products
being requisitioned or procured fall within and conform  with  PNDF,  Volume
I, current edition, pursuant to the above mentioned EO and AO.


      We also recommend to require the Municipal Health Officer  to  have  a
copy of the latest edition of PNDF, Volume I and ensure that  all  drugs  to
be purchased are included and listed therein to avoid  audit  disallowances.
If the drug to be requisitioned/procured is not included in  the  PNDF,  the
purchase of the drug should be supported with the written  approval  of  the
head of  National Drug Policy Office pursuant to the above regulations.


      Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to  prepare  an  adjusting
entry to take up  the Inventory Held for Distribution- Drugs  and  Medicines
for Distribution (Account 1-04-02-030) to present the  accurate  balance  of
the affected inventory and expense account in the financial statements.

Management’s Comment:

      The  management  had  committed  to  follow  the  regulations  on  the
procurement of medicines.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the LGU should ensure  that
all drugs to be procured are listed in the PNDF so that  there  will  be  an
assurance that it is safe to consume for the general public.

Recommendation:

      We recommend to the management the following:

   1. To prepare an accurate Annual Investment Plan that includes  all  the
      projects that are to be funded under the 20% Development Fund.


   2. To stop utilizing the 20% DF for payment of overtime services to avoid
      disallowance in audit.


   3. To strictly implement projects on the year it  was  appropriated  and
      ensure  that  utilization  is  in  accordance  with  DILG-DBM   Joint
      Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011.

Management’s Comments:

      The management in their letter reply to the  AOM  explained  that  the
mode of implementation of the Drainage System and the Barangay Road  Program
was undertaken by administration using the LGU  owned  heavy  equipment  and
the  available  engineering  personnel.   That  most  of   the   engineering
personnel specially the heavy equipment drivers/operators/helpers,  laborers
and the project supervisors  were  required  to  render  overtime  works  to
hasten the completion of the project.  The  management  has  considered  the
overtime payment as part of the labor component of  those  programs/projects
hence they allowed it to be charged to the 20% Development Fund.   Meanwhile
on the issue that the name of the project did not  coincide  with  the  name
reflected in the AIP and in the  Annual  Budget,  they  commented  that  the
data found in audit is not the approved budget.  However, they committed  to
abide with the recommendations of the Team.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the LGU should ensure  that
overtime pay should not be disbursed using  the  20%  Development  Fund,  to
avoid disallowance in audit.

Recommendation:

      We recommend to the management the following:

   1. To stop charging the payment for honoraria  of  MDRRM  Responders  and
      Warehouse Man since this is not allowed to  be  charged  to  MDRRM  as
      provided in Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2014-1 dated April  4,
      2014.

   2. To strictly observe the proper utilization of MDRRM in  order  not  to
      deplete the funds and in order to avoid disallowances in audit.

Management’s Comment:


      The management had committed to ensure that honoraria payment in  2017
of DRRM Responders shall be charged to the General Fund.


COA’s Rejoinder:

      We stand firm with our recommendation that the LGU should ensure  that
only proper charges be paid using the MDRRM fund to  avoid  disallowance  in
audit.

Recommendation:

      We recommend to the management to strictly observe the BIR regulations
on the withholding of taxes to avoid the sanction that shall be  imposed  to
the withholding agent for its failure to deduct the accurate taxes.




Management’s Comment:

      The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal  Treasurer  explained  that
there is  tax  exemption  of  3%  Final  Withholding  Tax  for  agricultural
products.

COA’s Rejoinder:

      We emphasized that the exemption for  VAT  is  only  allowed  for  VAT
registered suppliers.  The above mentioned supplier is a Non-VAT  registered
hence cannot availed of the said exemption.   The  LGU  should  ensure  that
accurate withholding of taxes in all government payments be imposed.
